pathophysiology_summary: Tinea corporis (dermatophyte infection of glabrous skin, often geophilic from soil play) produces expanding annular plaques with a raised scaly advancing border and central clearing; intense pruritus drives excoriation with mechanical pinpoint bleeding and weeping. Discriminators: the annular scaly advancing border with central clearing (and mechanical, not systemic, bleeding) distinguishes it from the hot, uniform, spreading induration of cellulitis/impetigo; the pruritus and epidermal scaling distinguish it from the smooth, asymptomatic plaques of granuloma annulare; and central clearing with a raised active border distinguishes it from uniformly inflamed nummular eczema. Management breaks the itch-scratch cycle: a topical antifungal (terbinafine) to and beyond the border, a brief (≈3-day) mild topical corticosteroid over the antifungal to settle intense itch/inflammation, an antihistamine and short nails, plus hygiene/no-sharing; a short oral antifungal is reserved for extensive/refractory disease. Safety-net for secondary bacterial infection.

differential.evidence_basis: Annular scaly advancing-border plaques with central clearing, expanding; soil play.
Screen for hot/hard spreading induration, honey crust, fever — bleeding here is mechanical.
Smooth, non-scaly, asymptomatic — not itchy scaly rings.
Uniformly inflamed coin-shaped plaques without central clearing.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Diagnose tinea corporis, screen for cellulitis, and break the itch-scratch cycle (antifungal + brief mild steroid + antihistamine + short nails).

differential.evidence_basis: Annular scaly plaques; mechanical bleeding, no cellulitis features.
No hot/hard induration, honey crust or fever — remains a safety-net.
Working diagnosis: topical antifungal past the border + brief mild steroid + antihistamine + short nails + hygiene; infection safety-net.

red_flags.description: Secondary bacterial infection of excoriated skin (hot/hard spreading swelling, honey crusts/pus, fever)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Raw scratched skin can superinfect and progress to cellulitis.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating a scratched-but-benign tinea as cellulitis (unneeded antibiotics/ED).
Antifungal alone without brief steroid/antihistamine/short nails, so scratching/bleeding continues.
Using the mild steroid for more than a few days (masking tinea).

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Topical antifungal past the border + brief mild steroid over it + antihistamine + short nails + hygiene; review/urgent care if secondary infection develops.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Over-treating as cellulitis, or failing to break the itch-scratch cycle.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Tinea corporis: topical antifungal to/beyond the border; a brief mild corticosteroid may be added for intense inflammation; oral antifungal for extensive disease; hygiene/no-sharing.
